questions.question: What is the primary architectural purpose of the Adapter design pattern?
options: To enforce global single instance creation.
To enable two classes with incompatible interfaces to collaborate by providing a bridging wrapper.
To securely isolate a separate database.
questions.answer: To enable two classes with incompatible interfaces to collaborate by providing a bridging wrapper.
questions.explanation: If your analytics provider requires parameters formatted as XML, but your internal system solely emits JSON objects, the Adapter transforms the payload in the middle.

questions.question: What is the most powerful feature of utilizing the Decorator pattern over standard Class Inheritance?
options: It allows you to organically layer and attach unique new behaviors sequentially onto individual objects at runtime, rather than statically locking behaviors into massive inheritance class hierarchies.
questions.answer: It allows you to organically layer and attach unique new behaviors sequentially onto individual objects at runtime, rather than statically locking behaviors into massive inheritance class hierarchies.
questions.explanation: With inheritance, if you need a Email+SMS+Slack notifier, you must build a completely rigid `EmailSmsSlackNotifier` class. With Decorators, you simply wrap objects into one another flexibly at runtime.

questions.question: Which Structural Design Pattern completely shields client code from the complexities of a massive, heavily nested subsystem by providing a single, simplified, high-level interface?
questions.explanation: The Facade Pattern acts as a simple front door to a complex inner system. You just call `Facade.execute()`, and it expertly coordinates the vast underlying complexity on your behalf.
